title: Hysteretic Acoustic Band Structures in Shape-Memory Composite Thin Rods
authors: R. Esquivel-Sirvent, B. Manzanares-Martínez, J. Manzanares-Martínez
abstract: We investigate the propagation of longitudinal elastic waves in one-dimensional periodic composite rods composed of alternating segments of a shape-memory alloy (NiTiCu) and a polymer spacer (Parylene C). In the thin-rod regime, the longitudinal phase velocity reduces to $c=\sqrt{E/ρ}$, which coincides with the regime in which the elastic modulus of NiTiCu has been measured directly through its acoustic response across the martensitic transformation. Using the standard transfer-matrix method along the heating and cooling branches of the transformation separately, we compute the Bloch band structure of the infinite periodic system and the transmission spectrum of finite composite rods. Because the elastic modulus of NiTiCu follows different paths upon heating and cooling, the same external temperature within the transformation interval corresponds to two different phase fractions and, consequently, to two different phononic spectra. The resulting hysteresis of the underlying material is thus transferred to the collective acoustic response of the periodic structure: stop-band edges trace closed loops in the temperature--frequency plane, and the transmission coefficient of a finite rod at a fixed temperature depends on the previous thermal history. We further show that the geometric filling fraction of the active segment provides a complementary tuning mechanism, modifying the width of the spectral hysteresis loops and the position of specific gap closures independently of temperature. These results illustrate how a first-order structural phase transition with intrinsic thermal hysteresis manifests itself in the dispersion relation of a periodic elastic medium.
summary: This theoretical study models acoustic wave propagation in composite rods containing a shape-memory alloy undergoing a first-order phase transition. It demonstrates that the material's inherent thermal hysteresis causes the resulting phononic band structure and transmission spectrum to become path-dependent. This highlights how macroscopic thermodynamic history can imprint itself onto the collective vibrational modes of a structured material.
main_problem: The study investigates how the intrinsic thermal hysteresis present in a shape-memory alloy (NiTiCu) influences the collective acoustic response of a periodic composite structure.
main_result: The material's path-dependent thermal hysteresis is transferred to the acoustic properties, causing the phononic band structure and transmission spectrum to depend on the thermal history (heating vs. cooling).
method: The analysis employs the transfer-matrix method to compute the Bloch band structure and transmission spectrum for the periodic superlattice.
model_or_system: The system is a one-dimensional periodic composite rod made of alternating segments of NiTiCu (shape-memory alloy) and a polymer spacer (Parylene C). Material properties are modeled as functions of temperature, exhibiting first-order phase transitions.
key_observables: Bloch band structure, transmission coefficient ($	au$), and the specific acoustic impedance ($Z$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), geometric filling fraction ($\phi$), and the material's elastic modulus ($E$) and density ($ho$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the thin-rod approximation, assuming acoustic wave amplitudes are insufficient to alter the phase fraction, and that the material properties are solely functions of temperature.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the temperature dependence of acoustic impedance showing hysteresis, and the evolution of the phononic band structure, comparing distinct spectra for heating and cooling paths.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing the material's intrinsic hysteresis (acoustic impedance) to modeling the infinite periodic structure using the transfer-matrix method, and finally examining the measurable, path-dependent transmission spectrum of finite composite rods.
why_interesting: The concept of path-dependent properties (hysteresis) manifesting in collective excitations (phonons) is analogous to non-equilibrium dynamics studied in open quantum systems or driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
